title: Coil-built open fruit basket
subtitle: Wide shallow bowl with open lattice sides formed from unblended coils
excerpt: A wide, shallow bowl built from air-dry clay coils arranged in an open lattice pattern on the sides, with a solid slab base. The gaps between coils give a basket-weave visual. Approximately 200 mm diameter and 80 mm tall. No kiln is needed. Suitable for displaying dry citrus, garlic, or onions.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on coil-building methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rolled rope of clay used as the structural building unit; in this project coils are arranged in an open lattice rather than being stacked and blended into a solid wall
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used here as the solid base of the basket
A creamy mixture of clay and water used as an adhesive to bond coil crossings and the coil base-join; the open lattice relies entirely on slip bonds at each crossing point
The technique of scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying slip before pressing them together to create a strong bond
The drying stage at which clay holds its shape firmly but remains slightly damp, allowing trimming and minor corrections
The drying stage at which all moisture has left the clay body and the piece is ready for painting and sealing
Fully dried but unfired clay work that is fragile and will dissolve if wetted before sealing

content.text: This basket-form bowl has a solid
slab
base and open lattice sides built from
coils

arranged in a weave pattern. Unlike a standard coil-built vessel, the coils are not blended into a solid wall. Instead they are laid diagonally in two directions and bonded at each crossing point with
slip

, leaving open gaps that give the piece its basket character. No kiln is required. Finished dimensions are approximately 200 mm diameter and 80 mm tall. The piece is decorative: it is not suitable for wet or damp produce.
Preparation

Prepare all your coils before beginning assembly. You will need approximately 30 coils each 8 mm in diameter and 250 mm long. Roll them on the board and cover them with a damp cloth to keep them pliable. Prepare slip by mixing a walnut-sized piece of clay into a small amount of water until thick cream consistency. Knead the base slab clay separately for two minutes before rolling.
Construction

Make the base. Roll a slab 8 mm thick and cut a circle 200 mm in diameter using the fettling knife and a card circle template. Place this on the wooden board. Smooth the top surface with a damp sponge.

Lay the first ring coil. Score the outer perimeter of the base top surface using the needle tool and apply slip. Bend a long coil into a circle to travel around the base perimeter and press it firmly into the scored, slipped base edge. This first coil is the bottom boundary of the lattice sides. Blend its inner edge downward into the base using a fingertip so it is securely attached. The outer edge of this coil remains unblended.

Begin the diagonal coils. Lay the first diagonal coil from one point on the bottom ring, angling upward at approximately 45 degrees across the side of the basket. The coil end rests on the bottom ring coil and the other end extends above the intended rim height (you will trim the ends flush later). Apply slip at the point where the diagonal coil crosses the bottom ring and press gently.
Continue laying parallel diagonal coils in the same direction. Space them approximately 20 mm apart (from centre to centre), going around the full circumference. Use the
score-and-slip
method at each contact point with the bottom ring. Allow coil ends to overhang the intended rim height.

Lay the second set of diagonal coils in the opposite direction (approximately 45 degrees the other way), creating a crossing lattice. At each point where a coil from the second set crosses one from the first set, scratch a small score mark into both coil surfaces at the contact point and apply a dot of slip before pressing gently. Do not apply so much pressure that the coils deform; a firm pinch at each crossing is enough.

Add the top rim ring. Once all lattice coils are in place, lay a final coil as a rim ring at the intended height of 80 mm. Score and slip both the rim ring and the top ends of each lattice coil where it meets the rim. Press the rim ring over the tops of the lattice coils and smooth the join on the inside of the rim. This ring provides structural stability to the open lattice.

Trim the overhanging coil ends. Once the rim ring is attached and the lattice has firmed slightly (about two hours), use the fettling knife to trim any coil ends protruding above the rim ring flush with its top surface. Smooth the cut ends with a damp finger.
Allow the assembled basket to reach the
leather-hard
stage before handling. At this point check every crossing point and press down any that appear loose. Apply fresh slip to any crossing where the bond looks suspect.
Drying
Leave the basket on the wooden board at room temperature. The open lattice structure allows air circulation, so drying is faster than for solid-walled pieces: 36-2 days to
bone-dry
in average conditions. The individual coils will lighten in colour evenly when fully dry. The
greenware
stage is particularly fragile for an open lattice form: the crossing points are the weakest areas. Do not apply any pressure to the sides until fully dry.
Finishing

Paint the base and coils with acrylic paint. A terracotta, natural ochre, or cream tone complements the basket aesthetic. Allow paint to dry for two hours. Apply two coats of PVA diluted 50:50 with water, working the brush through the open lattice to coat every coil surface. The PVA coat consolidates the crossing joints as well as sealing the surface.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even after PVA sealing. This basket is not suitable as a water vessel and should not hold damp or wet produce. It is suitable for displaying dry citrus fruits, garlic, or onions at room temperature. Keep indoors. Wipe with a dry cloth. Do not immerse in water.
Variations
Vertical-horizontal weave: lay the lattice coils at 90 degrees rather than 45 degrees for a squarer grid pattern across the sides.
Rectangular bread basket: use a rectangular slab base (240 mm by 150 mm) and build straight lattice sides. This creates a bread or fruit basket shape instead of a circular form.
Mixed coil thickness: alternate thick coils (12 mm) with thin coils (6 mm) in the lattice for a more varied texture that emphasises the weave pattern visually.

rows.problem: Lattice crossings come apart during drying
rows.cause: Insufficient slip was applied at the crossing points, or the coils were too dry when they crossed
rows.fix: At leather-hard, apply fresh slip to any loose crossing with a fine brush and press the coils together. Hold for 30 seconds or prop with a small wad of paper while it dries

rows.problem: Coils sag and the sides lose their angle
rows.cause: Too many coils were laid before the base and lower lattice had time to stiffen
rows.fix: Allow the lower half of the lattice to firm for 4-6 hours before adding the upper rows. Prop the lower coils gently against a jar or balled-up cloth while they stiffen

rows.problem: Rim ring cracks at the join
rows.cause: The rim ring was added when the lattice coil ends had already stiffened
rows.fix: Score and apply fresh slip to the coil ends, wet the rim ring surface, and press together. Allow to dry slowly away from air movement

rows.cause: The solid base dried faster than the coil lattice, creating tension
rows.fix: Fill the crack at leather-hard with slip and smooth. At bone-dry, use undiluted PVA as a filler before painting
